metadata.title: AI and Jobs Focus Group Transcript Analysis

facilitator_questions.text: What are your first thoughts and reactions after watching the documentary? Can you relate personally to any of the emotional or interpersonal issues shown?
Share a story about a time you tried to talk about AI with others. How did it go? Did you change your mind or someone else's?
After watching the video, have your feelings about AI and its impact on jobs changed? Why or why not?
What questions do you still have about AI after watching this video? What questions were answered?
Where were your reactions similar or different from others in the group, and why might that be?
Feedback on the documentary: Would you have kept watching? What would make it more enjoyable? Were any parts boring or confusing?
Would you share this video with others or subscribe to the channel? Who would you send it to?
What recommendations do you have for making this video more relevant to younger audiences?

themes.name: Silicon Valley Hype vs Reality
themes.description: Skepticism about AI companies overpromising and underdelivering

themes.name: Human Agency and Choice
themes.description: The idea that AI adoption is a choice made by humans, not an inevitable force

themes.name: AI Safety and Decision-Making Limits
themes.description: Concerns about AI's inability to handle complex moral and situational decisions

themes.name: Environmental Costs of AI
themes.description: CO2 emissions, computing power, and sustainability concerns

themes.name: Human Bias in AI Systems
themes.description: AI inheriting and perpetuating human biases (racism, sexism)

themes.name: Emotional Attachment to Work
themes.description: Jobs as identity and meaning, not just income

themes.name: Devaluation of Human Creativity
themes.description: AI undermining the value of human-made art and work

themes.name: Policy Responses to AI Displacement
themes.description: UBI, regulation, and systemic responses to job loss

themes.name: Generational Attitudes Toward AI
themes.description: Differences in how Gen X, millennials, and Gen Z view AI

themes.name: Tech Optimism as Ideology
themes.description: Uncritical enthusiasm for technology, gendered aspects of tech culture

themes.name: Hidden Labor Behind AI
themes.description: Exploited workers training AI systems (Mechanical Turk, overseas labor)

themes.name: AI Bubble and Sustainability
themes.description: Questions about whether AI businesses are economically sustainable

themes.name: Loss of Critical Thinking Skills
themes.description: AI making people unable to think, write, or create independently

themes.name: Distinguishing Human from AI
themes.description: Growing difficulty telling AI-generated content from human-made

themes.name: Masculinity and Tech Progress
themes.description: AI progress framed as inherently masculine, risk-taking culture

themes.name: AI Literacy and Understanding
themes.description: Most people don't understand how AI actually works

narratives.name: The Silicon Valley Illusion
narratives.description: AI companies make grandiose claims backed by 'proof of concept' demos (like a golf cart) but fail to deliver. The Embark company's failure exemplifies this pattern.

narratives.name: Reclaiming Human Agency
narratives.description: Against defeatist attitudes: humans choose to adopt AI, and we can choose not to. We drove trucks and made art before AI, and we can continue to do so.

narratives.name: The Myth of AI Objectivity
narratives.description: AI is presented as safer and more objective than humans, but it's built by fallible humans and inherits their biases. The veneer of objectivity masks real problems.

narratives.name: Progress at What Cost
narratives.description: The drive for AI 'progress' comes with hidden costs: environmental damage, job displacement, loss of creativity. We must ask if infinite progress is worth pursuing.

narratives.name: The Humanity Behind the Job
narratives.description: Jobs like trucking aren't just labor—they're sources of identity, pride, and meaning. Seeing the human side changes the calculus of automation.

narratives.name: Tech Culture as Masculine Risk-Taking
narratives.description: AI enthusiasm is gendered—associated with uncritical tech worship, Elon Musk fandom, and dismissing safety concerns as 'stupid.'

narratives.name: AI Creating New Problems
narratives.description: AI doesn't eliminate human labor—it creates new exploitative labor (data labeling overseas) while retaining all the old problems plus new ones like carbon emissions.

narratives.name: The Erosion of Human Capability
narratives.description: Reliance on AI is making people—especially young people—unable to think, write, or create independently. 160 out of 170 freshmen using AI for one assignment.

narratives.name: Demystifying the Black Box
narratives.description: When you understand AI is 'just' sophisticated linear algebra and probability distributions, the mystique fades and realistic assessment becomes possible.

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: At the end it just goes, oh, they retired the fleet in 2023. Why? I want to know more about that. The premise of the documentary is like AI is this huge thing and it's actually taking over trucking, but also they just retired the fleet and I don't know why.
excerpts.reply_reason: No prior excerpt; attach to question anchor.

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: What resonated with me is this struggle between automation and safety and then also losing jobs. AI has kind of become this 'oh my God, it's rising and we're losing everything.' And I think that's necessarily probably true, but the way my brain works is okay, let's say AI does rise, how do we stop gap this loss of jobs?
excerpts.reply_reason: Fallback to previous excerpt (turn-taking adjacency).

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: It reminds me of universal basic income. That's something I had been thinking about a lot with AI—if we're going to consistently lose what you would call 'low skilled' jobs to automation, how do you stop gap that? Especially because these people are talking about they're barely paid anything when they do have their jobs, what about when they don't?
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.50).

excerpts.speaker: jessie
excerpts.text: In econ class we talk about different forms of unemployment from technological advancements. When we read about it in the news or see it happening, we kind of always view it in a positive light. And sometimes behind that there are certain consequences to certain demographics who might be experiencing the lower end of having these advances.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=1.00).

excerpts.speaker: zahed
excerpts.text: I was not surprised the company shut down. That whole time I was wondering when he said 'less than half a decade.' It seems like a pretty clear cut case of a Silicon Valley company, an AI based Silicon Valley company making insane claims and not being able to back them up, making millions of dollars in funding and then losing it because their idea was not nearly as ready as they claimed.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=1.00).

excerpts.speaker: zahed
excerpts.text: He had a golf cart, he attached what looks to me like a motor to the steering wheel. Sure that's something, but as the driver in the second half pointed out, there's so many other factors. Suddenly you have to do computer vision, but then there are actually a hundred things you need to do and it's not nearly as simple as attaching a motor to a golf cart.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.67).

excerpts.speaker: zahed
excerpts.text: I had experiences before with that mentality of 'just look, this is our proof of concept, the golf cart. From there we can do self-driving trucks, just give engineers some money in a couple of years and it'll work out.' No, it does not. I think we're seeing that very often right now with most AI startups.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=1.00).

excerpts.speaker: victoria
excerpts.text: My mom got laid off of her job. It's not an insane job where she's getting 150K a year, but it was what she liked to do and loved to do. Not because of technology or AI, but because the company wasn't doing well. I understand when the truck driver said 'This is what I love to do.' Even though they're only making around $22,000 a year, that's not a lot, but it is what he loves to do.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.25).

excerpts.speaker: victoria
excerpts.text: I don't think AI and self-driving trucks are going to be a real thing very soon. There's a lot of decision making that self-driving cars can't do. Like if a kid was coming versus a car was coming, what direction would they steer? But whenever it does happen, there's a lot of emotional attachment to jobs and people are going to be very angry about losing their job despite what kind of income they had.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.50).

excerpts.speaker: breanna
excerpts.text: They mentioned how computers are safer than humans, but to what extent can computers account for human mistakes? Exactly with the child thing. I saw a headline yesterday about Google's CO2 emissions over the past five years—they've raised over 48% because of AI generation. That's really scary to me. Where do we value progress to how it is essentially shortening our lifespan?
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.33).

excerpts.speaker: breanna
excerpts.text: Especially where climate change is a huge threat to all of us right now—progress in the short term versus staying alive. It's interesting to see what human beings are valuing right now.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.50).

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: It reminds me of this game created by MIT called Moral Machine to collect data on what humans think is the most moral for an autonomous driving car. It would do things like 'if this car has to crash into someone, should it crash into an elderly woman or an elderly man?' That's scary. But also interesting because the truck driver said 'all of this is human made and humans make mistakes.'
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.40).

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: We have this veneer of objectivity with AI—'oh my God, it's a hundred percent safer.' That's what Alex Rodrigues was saying, this is how you cut traffic collisions and deaths. Maybe there's some truth that it might be safer. But that's also why we've had so many issues with algorithms being racist, being sexist. When Alexa and voice recognition was developed, it didn't recognize female voices because we only had male engineers on the team.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=1.00).

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: We get so caught up with endless advancement that we don't remember that we are humans and we're making this technology and we're still wrong a bunch. It was scary to see this guy be like 'Yeah, it's totally cool.' I was nodding when Zahed said it sounds like a Silicon Valley dude who got a bunch of money and decided he had proof of concept. The golf cart was very silly to me—I could drive a golf cart when I was 12, that doesn't mean I can drive a 48 wheeler.
excerpts.reply_reason: Explicit mention of speaker 'zahed' in text.

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: The CO2 emissions thing is something that AI has popped up and no one thinks about. The way AI works, it's not just something happening in the cloud. There's a bunch of servers working overtime. When you open Google Docs and it's like 'do you want to write this thing with AI?' All of that takes so much computing power. It's the same thing with NFTs—everyone was like NFTs are really bad for the environment because all of that computing power is actually screwing up our environment.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.67).

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: When you de-link the impacts of AI to what AI actually is, everyone's like 'oh, this is cool.' But there's this idea—do we actually need infinite progress or should we take a step back and say 'Hey, maybe this isn't cool.'
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.67).

excerpts.speaker: martin
excerpts.text: My debate coach has always talked about AI. He always calls it 'ChatGTP' and says it is going to ruin the world. He's very defeatist and pessimistic. It's an attitude I hear a lot from people in Gen X and older generations and it really bothers me. He's like 'Pandora's Box has been opened and AI is going to take all our jobs and ruin all this stuff.'
excerpts.reply_reason: Fallback to previous excerpt (turn-taking adjacency).

excerpts.speaker: martin
excerpts.text: A lot of people's attitude with AI is 'it's out so it's already over.' That attitude is so frustrating because what happened to solving problems? Yes, AI does cool things, but we did all those things before AI, we can do them again. We actually do know how to drive trucks without computers and we can make art ourselves and write things ourselves. We don't have to just accept that AI is taking all our jobs.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=1.00).

excerpts.speaker: zahed
excerpts.text: AIs don't take over jobs, humans do—they choose to replace human workers with AI. People can and will be driving things and making art as long as those things exist. But a lot of the choices about AI aren't ours—'ours' by the perspective of people who are at risk of being replaced. That's not their choice to make. That's a choice made by someone probably on the other side of the country in a boardroom. And that person will almost certainly be choosing the AI because it makes more money.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.67).

excerpts.speaker: amanda
excerpts.text: I was thinking about how AI is also being used to replace Uber drivers. I saw this clip on TikTok where this girl was trying out an Uber navigated by AI, there was no driver, and I was freaking out. I don't know if this is very trustworthy. We are basically putting human lives at risk with something that we're not even certain of ourselves. We're making decisions with the mindset of 'we're going to make progress and make things more efficient.' But there's so many other risks.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.40).

excerpts.speaker: victoria
excerpts.text: I had an experience talking with a male about self-driving cars. He was at school talking to some girls about it, and the girls were like 'I don't trust the self-driving cars.' And he said 'I just think they're so stupid. Why wouldn't you trust a car? You're actually an idiot if you wouldn't ride a self-driving car.' And I was like, whoa, hold on.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.20).

excerpts.speaker: victoria
excerpts.text: There's a certain demographic of people... Elon Musk fanboy, every time he sees a Cyber Truck he's like 'Oh my gosh, I need one.' A lot of people love technology so much that they're willing to believe anything about it and trust it completely. That's when it starts to get dangerous—if you're willing to put someone else down for trying to be safe. People who own companies will put it out there at the expense of other people's lives just to get some money.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=1.00).

excerpts.speaker: breanna
excerpts.text: I saw a Vox article that talked about how AI and progress with it is inherently masculine because of the risks. The progression and how we're choosing to replace things, how dangerous it is with risk taking. And Elon Musk—you think about the progress he's created with self-driving cars and AI, but also I think he is a terrible person.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=1.00).

excerpts.speaker: breanna
excerpts.text: In a world where we are moving towards AI and it's growing into our everyday lives, more people like Elon Musk—maybe that had a cool idea that changed a certain field—are coming into power and having more hold over things. It's scary. Maybe there'll be a good person that comes up with cool AI. But for now, all I'm thinking about is Elon Musk and the next Elon Musk.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=1.00).

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: There was this YouTube video by Drew Gooden called 'AI is Ruining the Internet.' He started making interesting points about how AI is essentially devaluing human creation and art—this idea that we can just feed a computer all of these things and it'll produce art.
excerpts.reply_reason: Fallback to previous excerpt (turn-taking adjacency).

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: He opens Getty stock images and this place where you're supposed to get a picture of a police car or a tree, you would also be seeing people selling AI generated images for 80 bucks. What's going on? We're devaluing what we understand human contribution to be. Especially ironic because the way AI works is you have to feed them human contribution—here's all these examples of human art and creation, now go make something that's inherently less valuable because it's just an amalgamation of all these things.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.67).

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: In the beginning I was like 'oh, trucking, maybe I get it.' When I'm on the freeway surrounded by four 18-wheelers, I'm scared. Maybe it would be better if it was automated. I'm from California with family friends in Silicon Valley, so I'm probably more open to technology. But when you see the human contribution side, talking to this trucker family—she was like 'we pay this much for gas, we do this for the parts, he's sleeping in the car.'
excerpts.reply_reason: Fallback to previous excerpt (turn-taking adjacency).

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: I never really considered trucking as an industry where people put their heart and soul into. But it shifted my perspective from the purely non-emotional 'yeah, it's bad, the jobs are getting taken away' to also 'this is something that someone can take as much ownership of as creating art.' It's just as important. Seeing the humanity behind it changed my mind. I was always pretty like 'no, taking jobs is pretty bad,' but it's shown a new aspect for me.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.67).

excerpts.speaker: zahed
excerpts.text: I got most of my preexisting beliefs validated. They were telling the woman in trucking 'Oh yeah, this company Embark is already doing self-driving trucking.' I was wondering if that's actually happening, maybe that's an interesting discussion. And then no, actually the company shut down because of all the reasons we were talking about. So that was not particularly shocking.
excerpts.reply_reason: Fallback to previous excerpt (turn-taking adjacency).

excerpts.speaker: zahed
excerpts.text: When it comes to computer vision problems, there are people paid to do work on training AI through programs like Amazon's Mechanical Turk. They are generally people overseas who do not have very many opportunities, and they're being paid very little. So we're not only taking from human artists, going down ArtStation scraping everything, but we're creating a whole new industry full of human people who are not...
excerpts.reply_reason: Fallback to previous excerpt (turn-taking adjacency).

excerpts.speaker: zahed
excerpts.text: Sometimes when it's fake, it's just people, and when it's real, it's also a lot of people in India. We kind of see AI as 'if we take the humans out of the equation, then those people aren't going to be in this industry anymore.' But we've created a new industry with all the carbon emissions. Every problem that we're trying to escape with AI shows up in the production of AI.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.67).

excerpts.speaker: victoria
excerpts.text: Every single aspect that we're trying to fix, there's a downside. Everything that involves AI, there happens to be a downside. When people think of AI, they might think of self-driving cars as a big problem. But the smallest things—students writing essays with AI—needs to be addressed.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.25).

excerpts.speaker: victoria
excerpts.text: At my school, all the freshmen—I think like 170 students—only 10 of them didn't use AI for this one assignment, and they all got caught. The teachers were so mad, some of them were literally crying because they spent so much time on these lesson plans and it felt like the students weren't putting the work in.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=1.00).

excerpts.speaker: victoria
excerpts.text: Freshmen, your work is not hard, and you have to write one paper on any topic of your choice, and you're choosing not to write it. We're actually making real life duds—people who cannot think for themselves because they're relying so much on this. Creativity is going down because people can't problem solve. It's so easy to do everything. Making things easier for people is the exact opposite of what it means to make change.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.67).

excerpts.speaker: martin
excerpts.text: I didn't really know much about AI in the trucking industry. The whole video is like these people weren't really aware about the problem and then they found out about this company doing it. But the conclusion being that the company shut down and retired its whole fleet confirmed a belief I had that AI is somewhat of a bubble. There's a lot of investment and excitement and proof of concept, people putting money in.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=1.00).

excerpts.speaker: martin
excerpts.text: A lot of these AI startups are not necessarily super sustainable—environmentally, but also just actually sustaining the business. One of my questions was: are these AI businesses really going to be completely taking over and taking all our jobs? We can't use this one example to write off AI's potential to damage the job market, but it's not looking good necessarily for AI.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.50).

excerpts.speaker: victoria
excerpts.text: My question that I'm still seeking an answer for: to what extent are we going to sacrifice our environment and our current problems for AI that is not helping anyone at the moment? Well, it's helping some people, but what extent are we going to sacrifice our lives for that?
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.25).

excerpts.speaker: amanda
excerpts.text: How do we determine what remains human and what remains AI? Both are very interchangeable and obviously influence each other, but we can't let AI take over everything. My brother did an original oratory on this—imagine walking into a doctor's office for surgery and they're like 'give me one second, ChatGPT is just helping me prep this surgery.' How would you feel in that situation?
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.25).

excerpts.speaker: amanda
excerpts.text: I'm worried that in the future, whether with writing essays or creating art, AI is literally just going to take over. At that point, we're not even going to be able to distinguish between what's actually created by humans and what's created by AI. That's a genuine concern.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.50).

excerpts.speaker: breanna
excerpts.text: A lot of my family members and family friends consistently reposting AI generated pictures and they don't even realize it. It's not even their fault to a degree—it's the accounts that create these crazy images because they know they're going to get reposted. Accountability is a huge thing. Tracing down an AI image would be extremely difficult. Where did it come from first? How do you hold people accountable?
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.67).

excerpts.speaker: jessie
excerpts.text: I watched a video about a girl who created an Instagram account that was completely AI generated. She said it was kind of easy to tell—seven fingers, background warped, trees not looking like trees. But she was shocked because people didn't immediately catch on. There were a lot of simps on Instagram. She realized a lot of people are actually profiting off AI generated pictures—creating OnlyFans with AI generation.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=1.00).

excerpts.speaker: jessie
excerpts.text: We have been very focused on thinking about how we can move forward with technology and where AI can conquer more industries. But in terms of how it really affects us as human beings, that's being put to the side in terms of thinking about how we progress.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.25).

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: We're all pretty similar. We have pretty similar reactions. That's kind of a product of the people here—a lot of us know each other, we're from generally similar backgrounds, same age, just graduated high school. I'm curious if we brought someone from Victoria's school who was in that freshman class, what they would think. Or that friend she was talking about, or even more guys.
excerpts.reply_reason: Explicit mention of speaker 'victoria' in text.

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: We're having this conversation about how progress and AI is inherently probably a little bit more masculine, which is interesting—I hadn't heard that before and I'm going to look into it more. But also having one guy here... A lot of us have said 'yeah, that video was cool, but it reinforced my beliefs.' I would love to talk more to people who are pro AI and hear about it.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.67).

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: Transparently, a lot of my beliefs on AI have come from self-conceived and self-built notions. I want to hear more because it can't just be the eight of us that are like 'yeah, AI sucks pretty much.' There has to be a reason, other than boardrooms and people saying 'this saves money.' There definitely is individual level buy-in to AI, and I want to know why.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.20).

excerpts.speaker: jessie
excerpts.text: A lot of people aren't that entirely knowledgeable about AI—the concept of how it works. Most people have a very basic understanding of what AI is and what it's generating, but not its mechanics. When I talk to my family members about AI, they use it as jargon in normal conversations and don't necessarily know the impact it actually has.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.25).

excerpts.speaker: zahed
excerpts.text: What is the buy-in for everyday people? We mentioned a bit before—this abstract notion of progress, it's a new shiny thing, an innovation. Also the ease of use. You can call it taking away creativity, the easy way out, which is true. But people are going to take the easy way out. In terms of individual buy-in, those benefits are pretty clear cut.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.25).

excerpts.speaker: zahed
excerpts.text: People who are optimistic towards AI, the idea I see most is 'it's going to have exponential growth.' Sure, AI images suck now, ChatGPT says false information, but none of this existed two years ago. Two years from now, who knows? If ChatGPT says false information 50% of the time now, if we cut that in half every two years, then at some point it'll be reliable enough for everyone to use.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.67).

excerpts.speaker: zahed
excerpts.text: Jessie mentioned people knowing how AI works under the hood. I have a passing amount of knowledge. The answer is it's a whole lot of linear algebra. If you see AI like the vast majority of people do, it's like a black box. You put stuff in and it gives you something. Who knows what it'll give you.
excerpts.reply_reason: Explicit mention of speaker 'jessie' in text.

excerpts.speaker: zahed
excerpts.text: When you go into the nitty-gritty, it's a lot of math, an algorithm. It's different from the usual thing we think of—it has a capacity for learning, which is interesting from a scientific and mathematical perspective. But after learning about that, every time I think about ChatGPT doing stuff, I think: it's going to turn all these words into vectors, do a bunch of layers, generate a probability distribution of what the next most common word is, choose something, and keep doing that until it selects 'end of prompt.'
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=1.00).

excerpts.speaker: zahed
excerpts.text: Having just a little bit of technical information about AI kind of bursts a lot of the illusions, because it's not a magic thing. When you see a technology having great results and seems to be improving, and a lot of people in powerful positions in Silicon Valley are optimistic, it's easy to think 'this is just going to work and I'm going to believe in it.' I don't blame people for that.
excerpts.reply_reason: Highest theme/narrative overlap in last 6 excerpts (Jaccard=0.67).

excerpts.speaker: martin
excerpts.text: I probably would've kept watching, but it was weird that there was no music. I'm just used to there being music in documentaries.
excerpts.reply_reason: Fallback to previous excerpt (turn-taking adjacency).

excerpts.speaker: aayushi
excerpts.text: If you're going to tackle a topic that's complicated, why cut it to 10 minutes? We've said five times, why did the company shut down?
